title: Client Proposal Writing
description: Write a complete client proposal including executive summary, problem statement, proposed solution, business case with ROI, implementation plan, pricing, and terms. Make it ready to send to a real buyer.
whyItMatters: The proposal is often the document that gets circulated internally at the buyer's company — to people you've never met. It needs to sell on your behalf when you're not in the room. A great proposal tells a clear story: here's your problem, here's the cost of the problem, here's our solution, here's the evidence it works, and here's the investment. The best AEs write proposals that make the decision easy.

steps.title: Define the Proposal Scenario
steps.description: Choose a specific deal scenario: company, buyer personas, pain points uncovered in discovery, competitive context, and budget range. Write a deal summary that captures everything you know about the opportunity.
steps.tips: Base this on your earlier project work if possible — use the same company and personas. This creates continuity and makes the proposal feel grounded in real discovery.

steps.title: Write the Executive Summary
steps.description: Write a one-page executive summary that a CEO could read in 2 minutes and understand the value proposition. Structure: current challenge, cost of the problem, proposed solution, expected outcomes, and investment required. No jargon, no fluff.
steps.tips: Write this last, even though it goes first. It's the most important page of the proposal — many executives only read this page. Every sentence should earn its place.

steps.title: Build the Problem Statement and Business Case
steps.description: Document the buyer's current state, the problems they face, and the quantified business impact. Then build a simple ROI model: Annual cost of the problem - Annual investment in your solution = Net annual value. Include payback period and 3-year total value.
steps.tips: Use the buyer's own numbers from discovery wherever possible. 'Based on the $180K in lost revenue you mentioned' is far more compelling than 'Industry benchmarks suggest companies lose $X.'

steps.title: Propose the Solution and Implementation Plan
steps.description: Describe your proposed solution — specifically what the buyer gets, how it addresses their pain points, and what the implementation looks like. Include a timeline with milestones: kickoff, configuration, training, go-live, first value review. Be realistic about timelines.
steps.tips: Map each solution component to a specific pain point. Don't describe features in isolation — connect every element to a business outcome the buyer articulated.

steps.title: Present Pricing and Terms
steps.description: Present pricing clearly with 2-3 options (good/better/best). Anchor the price against the cost of the problem, not against competitors. Include contract terms, payment schedule, and any conditions. Write a clear 'next steps' section with a specific call to action.
steps.tips: The pricing section should feel like a logical conclusion to the business case, not a separate topic. If the ROI is 5:1, the price feels like a bargain. If you present price without context, it always feels expensive.

deliverables: A complete proposal (8-15 pages) with executive summary, problem statement, solution, business case, implementation plan, and pricing
An ROI model spreadsheet with assumptions and calculations
A cover email to accompany the proposal

rubric.description: Concise, compelling, and self-contained. A senior executive could read it in 2 minutes and understand the full value proposition without needing the rest of the document.
ROI model is credible with clear assumptions, conservative estimates, and specific numbers. Payback period and multi-year value are calculated.
Every proposed solution component is explicitly tied to a pain point uncovered in discovery. Nothing is included that doesn't address a stated need.
The proposal is well-designed, error-free, and ready to share with a buyer. Formatting is consistent, language is professional, and the document tells a cohesive story.
